---
title: "Agentic redaction - tests and outputs"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "On this page"
---

# Summary

Redaction tasks are complex tasks that involve text and vision understanding, plus an in-depth review of results to create a final redacted document. Privacy is also key, which gives a strong incentive to use local, open source models if possible. I investigated using the possibility of using agentic workflows to conduct redaction tasks and subsequent document reviews to see if end-to-end redaction tasks are currently possible with open source models (as of late April 2026).

A skill file was developed for [document redaction](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/document_redaction_examples/blob/main/vlm_blog/agentic_redaction/skills/doc-redaction-app/SKILL.md){target="_blank"} and [modifying redaction results](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/document_redaction_examples/blob/main/vlm_blog/agentic_redaction/skills/doc-redaction-modifications/SKILL.md){target="_blank"} based on the open source [Document Redactions app / package](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/doc_redaction){target="_blank"}. The agents were instructed to redact an [example document](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/doc_redaction/blob/main/example_data/Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf) according to a set of [custom instructions](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/document_redaction_examples/blob/main/vlm_blog/agentic_redaction/skills/Example%20agent%20skills%20use%20prompt.txt){target="_blank"} by using a publicly-available [Hugging Face space](https://huggingface.co/spaces/seanpedrickcase/document_redaction){target="_blank"} serving the redaction app as a tool (this could be served locally).

Two models were tested. [A fine tuned version of Kimi 2.5](https://cursor.com/blog/composer-2-technical-report) was tested through the Cursor Agent Composer 2 model, which had some success at following instructions. The second model was chosen to fit within a commercial GPU (24GB VRAM), while having sufficient speed and performance to perform agentic tasks. Qwen 3.6 35B A3B was tested within Hermes Agent and had moderate success at the redaction task.

# Introduction

Redaction tasks are highly complex, and differ from many other types of work with documents in that it is a highly manual vision & text understanding task. To successfully redact a document, you need to understand and complete a number of tasks for each page:

1. Read and fully understand the text.
2. Determine the physical location of each word on the page.
3. Assess what is 'personal information' in the specific context discussed in the text.
4. Assess any images on the page to see if they also contain identiable information (e.g. faces, signatures) that also need to be redacted.
5. Accurately place redaction boxes on top of the content to be redacted so that they cover exactly the information to be excluded, without overlapping adjacent content.


On top of the above requirements, redaction tasks often also have additional constraints due to the fact that the data is by its nature highly sensitive. There is a clear incentive to reduce reliance on solutions that send data elsewhere and potentially expose this sensitive information. And so redaction + local models is a clear win.


This post documents the tests and outputs from the agentic redaction task. The task was to redact a document using the Document Redaction app hosted on Hugging Face Spaces: [seanpedrickcase-document-redaction](https://seanpedrickcase-document-redaction.hf.space){target="_blank"}.

The source document was the Partnership Agreement Toolkit PDF from the project examples: [Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/doc_redaction/blob/main/example_data/Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf){target="_blank"}.


The agent was given the following instructions:


```text {.bash, word-wrap: break-word}
Using the doc-redaction-app skill, redact this pdf document: https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/doc_redaction/blob/main/example_data/Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf using the redaction tool hosted at https://seanpedrickcase-document-redaction.hf.space. Use the paddle OCR method if that is available, or tesseract if it is not. Use the the Local PII identification method. Save the results to a folder in your workspace named 'output'.

Next, I would like you to check through the redactions with the doc-redaction-modifications skill. I would like you to use the output files from the redaction task to check through redaction results on each page, and remove / add / modify redactions according to these rules:

- Any redaction box related to general country names should be removed
- All redactions for Rudy Giuliani should be removed
- Redaction box sizings and positions should be checked visually to ensure they fully cover the relevant words
- Redactions should be added for any signatures
- All mentions of London, and 'Sister City' should be redacted
- Ensure that all remaining redaction boxes cover genuine PII and are not false positives
- Ensure that other genuine PII is not missed, and is covered by a redaction box.

As you go, ensure that you check the redaction box positions for accuracy on the page with image exports.

After you have completed your review, upload the updated files into the Redaction app to create new finalised outputs. Put these in the 'output_final' subfolder in your workspace.
```


I tried first using the Agent tab in Cursor to conduct this task, using the Composer 2.0 model. This model is [based on a finetuned Kimi 2.5 model](https://cursor.com/blog/composer-2-technical-report), which is a highly-performant open source model. This is a large model, and so is not practical to run locally, but gives an indication of what is possible with the current frontier of open source models.

Generated by the Cursor Agent after conducting the review:

# Overview

In this post, I document a full end-to-end redaction and review task completed against the Document Redaction app hosted on Hugging Face Spaces: [seanpedrickcase-document-redaction](https://seanpedrickcase-document-redaction.hf.space){target="_blank"}.

The source document was the Partnership Agreement Toolkit PDF from the project examples: [Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf](https://github.com/seanpedrick-case/doc_redaction/blob/main/example_data/Partnership-Agreement-Toolkit_0_0.pdf){target="_blank"}.

The objective was not just to run a single redaction pass, but to follow detailed instructions and verify box-level accuracy:

- Use Paddle OCR if available, else Tesseract.
- Use Local PII identification.
- Remove redactions for country names and for "Rudy Giuliani" (printed name).
- Add redactions for signatures.
- Redact all mentions of "London" and "Sister City".
- Check and correct box placement visually page-by-page.
- Re-apply reviewed CSV and produce final outputs.

# Skills and tools used

## Cursor skills

Two dedicated Cursor skills were used:

- `doc-redaction-app`: operational defaults for running `/doc_redact` and downloading Space artifacts.
- `doc-redaction-modifications`: review-CSV editing and `/review_apply` workflow guidance.

These skills were valuable because they encoded practical constraints that are easy to get wrong during automation, especially `handle_file(...)` usage rules and output download mechanics.

## Runtime and APIs

The core stack used in scripts:

- `gradio_client` for calling Space endpoints.
- `httpx` for downloading returned server paths via `gradio_api/file=...`.
- `csv` + `pathlib` for review file editing.
- `PyMuPDF` + `Pillow` for page render + redaction overlay image checks.

Endpoints used:

- `/doc_redact` for initial short-path redaction attempts.
- `/redact_document` as fallback when short-path artifacts were not returned.
- `/review_apply` for iterative correction rounds.

# What worked well

## 1) Fast iteration through review CSVs

The `*_review_file.csv` model is excellent for controlled edits. It enabled targeted filtering and additions without rerunning OCR each time. For this task, it made it possible to:

- remove specific unwanted entries (e.g., Giuliani printed name),
- add custom boxes for signatures,
- add targeted terms (London, Sister City),
- and preserve everything else.

## 2) Reliable apply loop with `/review_apply`

Once a corrected CSV was prepared, `/review_apply` consistently produced updated artifacts. This made the page-by-page loop repeatable and low-risk.

## 3) Visual QA with generated overlays

Rendering each redacted page and drawing the CSV boxes was the key quality step. It quickly exposed:

- box drift,
- over-redaction,
- under-redaction,
- and phrase misses on scanned pages.

# Where it was difficult

## 1) `/doc_redact` success message with empty artifacts

In this run, `/doc_redact` could return a success-like message but no downloadable output paths. That made it necessary to detect empty path returns and fall back to `/redact_document`.

## 2) Scanned-page term matching

A major lesson was that PDF text search is not reliable on scanned/image-heavy pages. The robust method was to use OCR word outputs (`*_ocr_results_with_words_*.csv`) and compose boxes from those coordinates.

This was especially important for terms like "Sister City" where OCR tokenization can vary (`SisterCities`, `city/sister`, punctuation variants).

## 3) Signature coverage is inherently manual

Signature boxes were best handled with manually refined coordinates, then validated via overlay images. Automated text-only logic was not enough to produce clean, precise signature masking.

# Final workflow that proved effective

The most reliable sequence was:

1. Run initial redaction (`/doc_redact`, fallback to `/redact_document`).
2. Download redacted PDF + review CSV + OCR outputs.
3. Generate page review images (overlay CSV boxes on rendered pages).
4. Edit review CSV based on explicit instruction rules.
5. Apply via `/review_apply`.
6. Re-generate review images and inspect.
7. Repeat until instruction-level acceptance criteria are met.
8. Produce final artifacts in a dedicated output folder.

# Outputs and reproducibility

Task scripts used during this workflow are in `scripts/`, including:

- `scripts/redact_partnership_toolkit_initial.py`
- `scripts/edit_review_csv_partnership_round4.py`
- `scripts/review_apply_partnership_final.py`
- `scripts/generate_review_images_partnership_final.py`

Final artifacts from the accepted pass were written under:

- `output_final/partnership_toolkit/final_apply_002/`

For convenience, I also copied task outputs to:

- `agentic_redaction/`

# Practical takeaways

- The Hugging Face Space flow is practical for real redaction-review work, not just demos.
- Accuracy comes from combining automation with explicit visual QA loops.
- The review CSV abstraction is powerful and script-friendly.
- Scanned pages require OCR-coordinate-first logic, not text-layer assumptions.
- For compliance-style tasks, "done" should mean both term-level correctness and box-level geometry verification.

# Conclusion

Overall, the tools were capable and productive for a nuanced redaction task with multiple custom rules. The main friction points were edge-case endpoint behaviour and scanned-page token variability, both of which were manageable once the workflow shifted to explicit fallbacks and OCR-driven box construction.

In short: the redaction app + review CSV + `/review_apply` loop is a strong foundation for instruction-following redaction workflows, provided that visual verification remains a first-class step.
